To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions being taken to address bullying in schools, including exclusion behaviour within the school setting.
answer_text: Schools are required to implement the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and the associated statutory guidance, which recognises exclusionary behaviours as forms of bullying. These behaviours are expected to be addressed through schools’ addressing bullying policies, preventative measures and wholeschool approaches to wellbeing and inclusion.Since the Act came into operation in 2021, schools have been required to record all instances of bullying behaviour, including motivations, outcomes and actions taken. Boards of Governors are responsible for developing, implementing and regularly reviewing their school’s addressing bullying policy, ensuring that appropriate action is taken to prevent bullying and respond effectively where incidents occur.The Department supports action at school level through statutory guidance issued in March 2021, which sets out clear expectations on prevention, intervention and recordkeeping. Schools are expected to promote inclusive school cultures, encourage pupil participation and take early action to address behaviours such as social exclusion.In addition, schools receive support and advice through the Education Authority’s Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team, which engages with schools to offer guidance on good practice, including approaches to tackling exclusionary behaviour. While its primary focus is on preventative education and promoting positive approaches, the Team also offers practical support to parents, carers, young people and schools when specific incidents occur. The Education and Training Inspectorate also reviews how schools are implementing strategies to address bullying as part of its inspection and engagement activity.Where concerns arise, responsibility for managing individual incidents rests with schools, through their established policies and complaints procedures. These actions, taken together, support consistent, effective approaches to addressing bullying, including exclusionary behaviour, across the education system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any inspections being carried out to the bridge on the Limavady Bypass, following media reports that it is at risk of collapse due to flood bank erosion.
answer_text: I am aware of recent media reports relating to riverbank erosion upstream of Roe New Bridge on the Limavady Bypass.  My officials undertook an inspection of the bridge and riverbanks on 14 April which did not identify any issues affecting the structure. The area of bank erosion is remote from the bridge, with works to repair the affected embankment expected to commence this summer.  In the interim, my officals will continue to monitor the extent of riverbank erosion and implement measures to protect the structure should it be necessary.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to address the growing shortage of essential medicines including co-codamol and aspirin.
answer_text: There are over 14,000 medicines licensed in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, the UK medicines regulator and other operators within the supply chain. Through national and local medicine shortage management groups, my Department is updated on any anticipated upcoming supply disruptions, including essential medicines. If there is a medicine supply disruption, there are well established tools and processes that can be applied both locally and nationally to ensure patients can have access to the treatments they need. This includes providing healthcare professionals with advice and guidance on the actions they can take to ensure patients can access appropriate treatments. These communications include National Patient Safety Alerts, Medicine Supply Notifications and recommending healthcare professionals registering with the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS) medicines supply tool. The SPS medicines supply tool provides up-to-date information on anticipated resupply dates, appropriate alternatives, clinical information and recommended actions to take based on the above advice. Advice and guidance for healthcare professionals have been provided regarding aspirin 75mg dispersible tablets and co-codamol 30mg/500mg tablets.Medicines can also be placed on a list of medicines that cannot be exported from the UK or hoarded. This ensures all medicines within the UK can be made available to patients. Both aspirin and co-codamol 30mg/500mg tablets have been added to this list. Other tools and processes that can be utilised include the importation of medicines from other markets and Serious Shortage Protocols. All these actions can be considered and applied where appropriate to anticipated supply disruptions. While there are well established tools and processes that can be applied, I recognise the impact these supply disruptions can have on patients and on Health and Social Care providers in Northern Ireland. Consequently, I had previously written to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting my support for wider collaboration to improve long-term supply resilience across the UK.DHSC published a document in August 2025, entitled ‘Managing a robust and resilient supply of medicines’ which aims to reduce the frequency of shortages and minimise the impact on patients when they do occur. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when abiraterone prostate cancer treatment will be widely available.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110

answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has temporarily suspended transport services delivered by JACE Medical. The Trust has subsequently corresponded with the provider to set out the information required and the necessary timeframe for a response. This correspondence was sent on 2 December 2025.In addition, JACE Medical have been suspended from the non-emergency ambulance framework.  The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) has sent formal correspondence and subsequent updates to JACE Medical regarding the suspension.Both NIAS and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust have reallocated planned JACE activity to other providers in line with the regional framework to ensure continuity of service during their suspension. There has been no reduction in ambulance provision. NIAS has redistributed activity across existing providers and operational resources to maintain essential cover and support emergency department flow.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme was initiated on a phased approach. The first phase launched on 2nd June 2025 and granted reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (Rol). The second phase launched on 15th September 2025 and extended access to the wider European Union. Furthermore, the qualifying scheme eligibility criteria has also been reduced from 2 years to 1 year for those on an inpatient or treatment waiting list to further assist with reducing the waiting times within HSC services in Northern Ireland. There is other work ongoing with Independent Sector Providers in Northern Ireland as part of a package of initiatives to tackle waiting lists. From the Scheme launched in June 2025 and up until 17th November 2025, there has been a total of 548 applications received; 430 of those applications have been successfully approved and 7 have not been approved due to applicants submitting retrospective claims for surgery completed before the Scheme was launched. The remaining 111 applications are pending for a variety of reasons, but primary because the clinical evidence to support their application is outstanding. An allocated amount of £10 million has been awarded to the scheme, of which £3.98 million has been committed to date including a total of £219k for completed treatment payments. Applications received relate largely to orthopaedics which are 84% of the overall applications to date and from these, the majority are for knee replacements. Other key areas include general surgery, ENT, gynaecology and ophthalmology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans to introduce a child winter heating payment.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce a child winter heating payment. However, I do plan to bring a new Fuel Poverty Strategy for Northern Ireland to the Executive for approval in the very near future. This Strategy is focused on addressing fuel poverty in the round and will aim for a warm, healthy home for everyone. It will prioritise long-term, sustainable solutions aimed at improving energy efficiency and reducing fuel poverty across all households.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the benefits to Northern Ireland in hosting matches at the FIFA Women's World Cup in 2035.
answer_text: The FIFA Women’s World Cup is recognised as the largest single-sport event for women globally. With its overall potential to attract over 1.85 million unique ticket holders, more than 800,000 overseas visitors, and an international viewing audience exceeding 2 billion people, we stand to benefit greatly by our participation. Staging the tournament will further the growth of women’s football and will leave a lasting legacy of health and wellbeing for all, especially for women and girls. It will allow us to show we are a world class destination that can deliver iconic international events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action Health and Social Care Trusts are taking to identify army veterans in need of assistance who face barriers coming forward and who fear their identities could be realised.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are aware of the sensitivities around veterans' identity in Northern Ireland. Working in conjunction with the Northern Ireland Veterans Commissioner's Office and the community and voluntary sector they employ several measures to help protect veterans who may fear their identities being revealed when seeking healthcare. For example, veterans can access dedicated medical services, including physiotherapy and psychiatric/psychological interventions, through trusted providers experienced in treating ex-service personnel. Initial assessments are conducted confidentially with follow-up care closer to the veteran’s home. The Defence Medical Welfare Service offers mental health and welfare support to veterans and their families, including those dealing with PTSD, isolation, or anxiety. Their trained welfare officers work within clinical settings to provide non-clinical, confidential support, helping veterans navigate healthcare without fear of exposure. Veterans Welfare Service Northern Ireland offers professional and confidential support through Welfare Managers across the region, helping veterans access emotional, moral, and practical support without compromising their privacy. The service is designed to be trusted and discreet, ensuring veterans feel safe when seeking help.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to ensure equitable access to (i) Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP) monoclonal antibodies; and (ii) CGRP receptor antagonists, for migraine patients.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.Within this context, there are a range of Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide (CGRP) monoclonal antibodies CGRP receptor antagonists for the treatment of migraine which are  available in Northern Ireland. Individuals seeking access to these treatments should discuss their clinical circumstances with their hospital consultant in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action his Department is taking to address violence against hospital staff; and (ii) what discussions he has had with the Minister of Justice regarding attacks on health and social care staff.
answer_text: I recognise and acknowledge the issues around Violence and Aggression that Healthcare staff have to deal with in the course of carrying out their duties. Any form of violence or aggression towards staff is unacceptable and my Department will continue to work with Employers and Trade Unions to ensure that appropriate protections are in place for staff.HSC trust employers aim to provide a safe environment for all staff and service users. Each trust has dedicated security staff who maintain static posts and perform routine patrols to try and deter incidents but also to urgently respond to all requests for assistance with violent and aggressive persons as best possible. Trusts also have CCTV and access control systems in place as an additional deterrent to deny unauthorised entry. Security staff all receive the same best practice safety intervention training to de-escalate situations in the first instance and also on how to perform restraints as safely as possible for all involved as a last resort.Every service is required to carry out their own internal risk assessments and seek practical advice for management of aggression teams to identify training needs and additional support. This can range from basic courses in disengagement techniques to skilled courses in how to deescalate situations and perform restraints in a safe manner. This can also help identify if staff need any personal protective equipment (PPE) to help protect them. Some examples of this would be body armour, slash / spike & bite resistant gloves or sleeves, protective glasses and panic alarms.As each Healthcare Trust has robust policies and processes developed locally in relation to dealing with such incidents I have not had discussions with the Minister of Justice. But each trust also engages closely with the PSNI to ensure that any evidence held by trusts can be used in enforcement proceedings against individuals who perpetrate violence towards staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an assessment of the clarity of the guidance provided to clubs for the Northern Ireland Football Fund; (ii) any feedback received regarding the clarity of this guidance; and (ii) an assessment of his Department’s outreach strategy.
answer_text: I visited a wide range of clubs and met key stakeholders, and in parallel my Departmental officials worked closely with stakeholders to develop policy proposals for The Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF). Stakeholder feedback was taken on board, including the overwhelming view that clubs wished to have the opportunity to make their case for investment on a level playing field. On 30 January 2025, alongside the link to the application portal, clubs received an electronic copy of the Application Guidance and Scoring Matrix and the link to the DfC website where all documents relating to the Performance Programme could be found.  On 31 January 2025 an invitation was issued to all eligible clubs to attend one of three evening information sessions taking place 4th, 5th and 6th Feb 2025. All clubs were able to select their preferred session.  Subsequently all eligible clubs received an electronic copy of the presentation delivered at these sessions.On 4 March 2025 all eligible clubs received a link to an FAQ document which was developed based on questions asked at information sessions or via email. Applicants continued to avail of the opportunity to contact TNIFF officials when further clarification was required prior to the application closure date, and the FAQ document was updated to reflect additional questions.  Positive feedback was received from clubs and other stakeholders on the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department intends to carry out any assessment of the potential (i) economic; and (ii) community impact on the North West of local clubs not receiving funding from the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: Well in advance of the call for applications a Rural Needs Impact Assessment) (RNIA) was completed on The Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF) Performance Programme. This assessment recognised that TNIFF’s benefits were expected to span a wide range of Programmes for Government themes but also noted that TNIFF was not intended to disburse funding in such a way as to secure a regional spread; rather all applications would be subject to the same scoring matrix with no advantage or disadvantage given to rural clubs. Decisions were therefore taken on an assessment of need and merit rather than to achieve geographical spread. There is no evidence that need is equally distributed across all geographic areas. The RNIA was published on the Department for Communities website in October 2024. A copy can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-northern-ireland-football-fund-performance-club-strand-rnia.PDFGiven the level of need that has been demonstrated, I hope that the Northern Ireland Football Fund will be a rolling Programme and that will extend to all performance clubs, regardless of their location within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many clubs in the North West did not move to the next phase of the Northern Ireland Football Fund due to technical or administrative errors rather than the quality or merit of their proposals; and (ii) whether they were given a chance to rectify these errors.
answer_text: No clubs from the North West were prevented from progressing to the next stage of the Northern Ireland Football Fund Performance Programme as a result of technical or administrative errors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) support mechanisms; and (ii) feedback being offered to football clubs that did not move to the next phase of the Northern Ireland Football Fund, to help them strengthen future applications or access alternative funding sources.
answer_text: The clubs not selected in the prioritised cohort have been offered the opportunity to contact officials to arrange a meeting to discuss their applications and receive feedback. It is hoped that these clubs will be brought forward to a further stage of the Programme if additional funding is secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department engaged with clubs prior to the application deadline for the Northern Ireland Football Fund to ensure they (i) understood the requirements; and (ii) had adequate support in completing their applications.
answer_text: Application Guidance and the Scoring Matrix were sent to eligible clubs. This  was also made available online on the Department for Communities website https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-football-fund-performance-programme-documents Application Information Sessions were held on the 4th, 5th and 6th Feb 2025 across the region. Clubs were offered the opportunity to contact TNIFF officials by email if further clarification was required prior to the application closure date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any actions his Department is taking, alongside the Housing Executive and housing associations, to address anti-social behaviour.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions her Department is having around carrying out work in the evening to complete the ongoing active travel scheme at Clooney Road, Ballykelly.
answer_text: This significant investment on the A2 Clooney Road is part of my commitment to enhance and expand our active travel network across the north.   On completion, the scheme will provide significantly enhanced cycling and pedestrian facilities between Ballykelly and Greysteel. This is part of a long-term plan to improve active travel facilities on the north west and boost local tourism.My officials are investigating all options aimed at completing the works as efficiently as possible, balancing issues such as additional disruption to residents from night time working, safety of site operatives, cost, weather conditions, and the quality of finished product.The contractor is already working under restricted hours of 09.30 to 16.30 and to mitigate disruption to commuters further, they have reduced the length of the works and are manually operating traffic signals, when two way flow cannot be maintained through the works. Work was also programmed to ensure that traffic management was not required during the 3 week embargo for the Open and traffic management was removed for an additional 3 days to manage increased traffic as a result of the Foyle Cup.I appreciate that traffic delays are frustrating for motorists and would ask the travelling public to allow extra time for their journey as we aim to complete the project as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of written responses received to the 14 week consultation on proposals to transform general surgery in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the number who responded to oppose centralising emergency general surgery to Antrim Area Hospital.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the action she is taking to help establish a direct air route from the United States of America to Northern Ireland to capitalise on golf tourism.
answer_text: My Department is committed to enhancing air connectivity and capitalising on our tourism offering. We are developing an aviation policy informed by the 2024 York Aviation Report, which highlights key international destinations, such as New York, as drivers of inbound tourism. Officials are working closely with the aviation sector, other government departments and agencies.  A public consultation is planned for year-end. Through our 10-Year Tourism Vision and Action Plan, we aim to leverage world-class attractions, renowned golf courses, exceptional hospitality, and a strong track record in hosting global events to achieve our strategic goal: exceeding £2 billion in overnight visitor tourism expenditure by 2035.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of East West Council meetings his Department has participated in; and (ii) the number of North South Ministerial Council meetings his Department has participated in, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: To date, my Department has participated – through Ministerial attendance – in eight meetings of the North South Ministerial Council over the last five years, as detailed in the table below:2021Health Sectoral1Food Safety Sectoral12024Plenary 2Health Sectoral 1Food Safety Sectoral12025Health Sectoral1Food Safety Sectoral1No NSMC meetings were held between February 2022 and February 2024, due to the collapse of the Executive.Since its establishment in February 2024, two meetings of the East-West Council have taken place.  My Department did not participate in these, as the Council has a strong focus on economic development, with no obvious input required thus far in relation to health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline why furniture and school equipment remains in the unused former Coleraine Girls High School at Lodge Road, given the risk of theft.
answer_text: While the Education Authority (EA) owns the former Coleraine Girls High School site, it was most recently occupied by Coleraine Grammar School.As a Voluntary Grammar, the furniture in the school building belongs to them and it is up to them to make arrangements for its future use.I have asked the EA to contact representatives of Coleraine Grammar School to identify a suitable mechanism to have the remaining furniture and equipment removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the number of children with special educational needs that are without a school place at the start of the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: As of 5 September 2025, the Education Authority (EA) has advised the Department that there are no unplaced children with a statement of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's consideration to reopen the railway line to Limavady town, including the progress any plans.
answer_text: Following on from the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, Translink has undertaken a preliminary study to identify the rail infrastructure requirements for a new station at Limavady. This would require a spur off the main line and additional passing loops. The project is currently considered as a longer-term initiative in Translink’s capital plan, but its future requirements will be considered as part of the Belfast to Derry Enhancement Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department intends to pause roadworks on the A2 Clooney Road, Ballykelly during the O'Neills Foyle Cup.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to pause roadworks on the A2 Clooney Road, Ballykelly during the O’Neill’s Foyle Cup.Officials have been in contact with the Foyle Cup committee advising that advance notifications should be issued to those attending, to ensure that they plan their journey appropriately and leave adequate time to account for some traffic delay.My Department and contractor will do everything possible to minimise disruption during this period while still ensuring that work can progress on site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the future of Limavady courthouse.
answer_text: Limavady Courthouse has been closed since March 2020, with its business currently transferred to Coleraine. Prior to its closure, the building operated as a Hearing Centre and was opened for one day a week for the delivery of Magistrates’ Court business only.The NICTS Estate Strategy, published in December 2023, provides a long-term framework for investment in the Courts estate over the next decade and beyond.To implement the Strategy, NICTS is using data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes, assess the adequacy of its buildings, and in the context of the finite resources available, develop prioritised investment plans that will be subject to public consultation, as appropriate.The future use of Limavady Courthouse forms part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a passenger footbridge will be considered as part of the proposed plans to expand the park and ride facility at Bellarena Train Station, County Londonderry.
answer_text: Design planning for this project is at an early stage, however a pedestrian foot bridge is not considered to be a necessary requirement. The proposed new Park & Ride is located on the same side as the existing platforms, therefore safe compliant access to both platforms will be provided. Looking ahead, the funding of an effective public transport service is a key priority for my department. While I am keen to deliver what I can within the remainder of my mandate, I will need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available. This is likely to require some difficult decisions and bearing down on costs will be essential to protect essential public service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff employed by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust who have been subject to a disciplinary procedure in the last two years.
answer_text: The table below sets out the number of staff employed by Belfast HSC Trust who have been subject to a disciplinary procedure within the last two years, broken down by the number of formal and informal procedures.Disciplinary Proceedings InformalFormalTotalMAH CCTV Investigation Support Team 10885193Employee Relations Team357152509Total 465237702The above figures include a combined total of disciplinary cases managed by the Employee Relations Team, Human Resources Department, as well as the dedicated Muckamore Abbey Hospital (MAH) CCTV Investigation Support Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a recruitment and retention premium is applied to attract surgeons to work in the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Recruitment and Retention Premia (RRP) are in place in a number of Health and Social Care Trusts. There are mechanisms in place for a Trust to submit an application to my Department to apply an RRP to fill a specific post. Consequently, RRP are used to attract consultants to specific posts within a Trust rather than to a specific hospital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department intends to pause the roadworks on the A2 Clooney Road, Ballykelly, during the period of The Open at Royal Portrush Golf Club in 2025.
answer_text: Work involving traffic control on the A2 Clooney Road, Ballykelly will be suspended during the period of the Open Golf from the 13th to the 20th July.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the action her Department is taking to carry out essential maintenance on road signage in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Road safety is a key priority for my Department, and I am fully committed to reducing the number of accidents on our roads. Road signs serve an important function in conveying information and requirements about the road and there is no doubt that poorly maintained road signs can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted.The frequency of these inspections and the subsequent response time depends on the type of road and the volume of vehicular or pedestrian traffic. Any defective, damaged or missing signs identified through these inspections, or notified to us by members of the public, will be categorised as urgent or non-urgent work.Within the confines of Limited Service, road sign repairs are prioritised to address those posing the most serious risk to road users such as missing STOP and Give Way signs. Repairs on other damaged regulatory signage necessary for continued road safety are also carried out subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans her Department has to resurface Rathmore Road, Limavady.
answer_text: Structural Maintenance works are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our infrastructure in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including the A371 Rathmore Road, Limavady, due to limited budget resources, work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2025/26 financial year will be included within my Department’s Annual Report to Councils.In the meantime, officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health with reference to the Royal College of Radiologists report Clinical Oncology Workforce Census 2024, how he intends to ensure that patients do not face delays to the start of their Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy and radiotherapy treatments.
answer_text: In general, the majority of patients in Northern Ireland do not face long delays to the start of their Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT) and radiotherapy treatments.  The timings to commence SACT and radiotherapy treatments are generally within the recommended clinical timeframes.  Oncology services for some specialty services can experience delays for some new patient referrals due to a demand and capacity gap. My department is cognisant of the findings contained within the Royal College of Radiologists' (RCR) Clinical Oncology Workforce Census 2024 indicating urgent need to address clinical oncology workforce capacity across the UK.  I am acutely aware of the need to increase the number of speciality training places in Northern Ireland, as well as to expand the capacity of our existing workforce to train the next generation of oncology professionals.  This is essential to meet the ever-growing demand for both radiotherapy and systemic anti-cancer therapy treatments.Since 2019/20, a key priority of my Department is to address ongoing resilience issues with regard to the provision of outpatient assessment and the delivery of Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT).  By 2024/25 the Department invested a total of £10.8m across all five health and social care Trusts for the Oncology & Haematology Stabilisation Plans.   This has funded 167.76 whole time equivalent (WTE) additional staff across a range of health professionals to improve consultant capacity and skills mix in oncology and haemato-oncology services.  In addition, £2.5m recurrent funding has been identified in the 2025/26 Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan to help complete stabilisation plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, with reference to the Royal College of Radiologists report Clinical Oncology Workforce Census 2024, how he plans to reduce the reliance of Health and Social care Trusts on outsourcing of radiology reporting, given their forecast that outsourcing spend could reach as much as £10.5 million per year by 2029.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the merits of investing in more clinical oncology and clinical radiology speciality training places rather than relying on expensive outsourcing to private firms.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to accelerating an Northern Ireland Imaging Academy to increase the numbers of clinical radiologists being trained.
answer_text: Establishment of a multi-professional regional Imaging Academy is a strategic commitment for my Department and the answer to addressing current challenges in our Health and Social Care imaging services.  By providing a continuous supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing, the Academy will deliver an overall increase in imaging capacity within HSC for the benefit of all clinical specialties and support the reduction of excess waiting lists for diagnosis and treatment, as well as meeting the demands from unscheduled care.I am committed to progressing work on the Academy as soon as possible within the current financial climate and a business case is being developed by the Belfast HSC Trust.  However, the development and any acceleration of it remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Cancer Networks, role in developing local and regional long-term workforce plans to meet the demands they face.
answer_text: All workforce planning for health and social care in Northern Ireland follows the principles of the Regional HSC Workforce Planning Framework.HSC Trusts are responsible as employers for local, operational workforce planning.  This includes service delivery and safe staffing levels, operational vacancy management and recruitment, skill mix/band mix changes, career progression pathways and workforce design to support new models of care within organisations.My Department leads regional, strategic workforce planning.  This includes all of the HSC and the independent, voluntary, community and private sectors.  The aim is to secure workforce supply across the entire HSC over a 5 – 10-year horizon, while encouraging opportunities for workforce development.  The Department works in partnership with key stakeholders across the health and social care system, professional bodies, employee representatives and service users.The Northern Ireland Cancer Network contributes to both operational and strategic workforce planning through its constituent Clinical Reference Groups.  These contributions include:identifying workforce gaps in key specialist professional areas;collaborating to inform multi-year workforce planning, including projections for new consultant posts; andsupporting education and training initiatives to enhance capacity and promote a multidisciplinary skill mix.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any recent discussions they have had with the UK Government in relation to building global economic, cultural and tourism connections.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work carried out by his Department to assess the demand for a commissioned ADHD service, including how far advanced this work is and an expected completion date.
answer_text: The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  This work is on track for an estimated completion date of end June 2025, with significant progress made to date, including a literature review, assessment of best practice in other areas and extensive stakeholder engagement to inform the report findings.The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the potential implications for livestock numbers of the Climate Action Plan being prepared by his Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department expects the work to repair the steps from Mark Street to Kerr Street, Portrush, to be completed.
answer_text: I have been advised that the ongoing repair works to the steps between Mark Street and Kerr Street, Portrush are to be completed by 4 July 2025, subject to favourable weather conditions and no unforeseen delays.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans her Department has to ensure utility companies carrying out excavation work on the road network reinstate the surface with width to width resurfacing.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.The specification details the materials to be used and the standards of workmanship to be observed.If an undertaker does not reinstate the street in a satisfactory manner, my Department applies the procedures set out in the Code of Practice for Inspections.Undertakers remain responsible for the permanent reinstatement for a minimum period of two years, increased to three years for a deep excavation.There is no requirement for an undertaker to carry out full width resurfacing of the street beyond the immediate area of their excavations. That said, I recognise the impact utility work has on the life of our road infrastructure and my officials will be considering this going forward.My Department has issued a Code of Practice for the Co-ordination of Street Works and Works for Road Purposes. The code sets out the objectives of the co-ordination function, which seeks to minimise inconvenience to people using the street and to protect the structure of the street and the integrity of the apparatus within it.My Department and the utilities have a duty to co-ordinate their works in accordance with this code..A recent NIAO audit report highlighted a number of areas for consideration in relation to road openings by utilities and my Department is working to address their recommendations.In addition, my Department will be taking forward further work in relation to their responsibilities for the management of street works by utilities and others to ensure that the impact of the disruption to the road network and future maintenance is minimised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Community Resuscitation Strategy for Northern Ireland (2014) will be reviewed.
answer_text: The Community Resuscitation Strategy was launched in July 2014 by the then Minister for Health, Edwin Poots, with the aim to increase the survival rate for those who suffer an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, to the highest level that can be achieved across Northern Ireland.  The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) is leading on the successful implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators.  There are no plans to review the strategy at this time.NIAS has worked with the British Heart Foundation to develop ‘The Circuit’ (https://www.thecircuit.uk/) which is a national online defibrillator network linking to every ambulance service in the UK.  NIAS can readily provide information on AEDs registered on ‘The Circuit’ to 999 callers, should they require access.There are currently 3,708 Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) available to the public and registered with NIAS on ‘The Circuit’.  NIAS Community Resuscitation Team continue to be involved in activities within communities which will strengthen the chain of survival, and NIAS are installing 36 publicly accessible defibrillators outside NIAS premises across the province.There are currently 340 Community First Responder (CFR) volunteers and 22 schemes in NI.  NI also has over 4,000 members of the public who have a minimum of Basic Life Support training and have registered to be a GoodSAM responder. Since members of the public went live on GoodSAM in February 2023 over 140 people have accepted a GoodSAM alert.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any plans his Department has to fund a national Palliative Care Clinical Lead.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of palliative and end of life care in supporting people with palliative care needs to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.The Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, co-led by my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency, continues to drive developments and improvements in palliative care both regionally and at local level.As part of the structure of the Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, its Clinical Engagement Group provides a forum for palliative care professionals across disciplines to share good practice and guidance and to provide input to the development and implementation of the PCiP’s palliative care programme and work plan.  The Clinical Engagement Group is Chaired by the Palliative Care Nurse Consultant at the Public Health Agency. The PCIP Programme Board includes representation from the Clinical Engagement Group to represent professionals working in palliative care at Programme Board level in taking forward the Programme’s priorities.  The key priorities of the Palliative Care in Partnership Programme for 2023-26 are:Early Identification and Timely Intervention: to improve the early identification of people who could benefit from a palliative care approach; Co-ordination and Quality Improvement In Palliative Care Services: focussing on quality improvement initiatives and supporting localities and providers to co-ordinate services;Palliative Care Education and Training to promote competence and confidence in staff caring for people approaching the end of life across all care settings; andPublic Health Approach To Palliative Care: encouraging open discussion about death and dying and promoting advance care planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when her Department intends to undertake a reassessment of the preferred route for a bypass of Ballykelly that was originally announced in 2010.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing a range of Transport Plans, including the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan (RSTN TP), which will consider strategic roads, the buses which run on them and the rail network across the north. This transport plan will also consider the A2 Ballykelly bypass.If such a scheme were to be prioritised through the Transport Plans, my officials would need to undertake a reassessment of the route.  Indicative timescales for such a reassessment are in the region of 6-9 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department intends to take to alleviate the traffic delays as a result of the ongoing road works on the A2 Clooney Road between Ballykelly and Greysteel, specifically at times when no work is taking place.
answer_text: My officials are working with the contractor to ensure that the work is carried out as efficiently as possible while maintaining safety for both site operatives and the travelling public. The contractor has been given permission to use temporary traffic signals and has been encouraged to use a system which allows two-way traffic under a temporary speed limit when work activities permit. I would also advise that in the interest of the safety of the travelling public, the temporary traffic management may need to remain in place at certain times to protect from potential hazards in the works area, even when the contractor is not physically working on the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any funding opportunities his Department has to support the running of agricultural shows.
answer_text: The Executive agreed the final budget for 2025-26 for my department on 03 April 2025.  This is an extremely difficult outcome for DAERA and insufficient to meet the significant pressures it faces. I will therefore have to prioritise allocations against the delivery of statutory obligations and contractual commitments as well as priorities within the Programme for Government. Provision of funding for local shows is not a statutory obligation under the Agricultural Act 1949 (NI), and opportunities for financial support in the form of discretionary sponsorship (as per Section 12 of the Act) are extremely limited.One such potential opportunity for funding exists under the NI Regional Food Programme (NIRFP), delivered by DAERA. This scheme provides funding for the promotion of local food and drink at regional fairs and exhibitions, which would include agricultural shows. Applications to the NIRFP are now open for projects taking place between 1 May 2025 and 31 March 2026. Applications must be submitted by 4:00pm on Wednesday 16 April 2025. Awards will be subject to budget availability. Further information can be obtained on the DAERA website (https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-regional-food-programme).I met with representatives of the Northern Ireland Shows Association (NISA) and Irish Shows Association (ISA) on 01 July 2024 and encouraged them to engage in strategic discussions about funding for local shows with their local councils, drawing on the related findings of the Independent Resilience Review in support of their applications for funding.Show organisers may also consider wish to consider signing up to the DAERA Grants and Funding Text Alert Service and also visiting the Grants and Funding hub on the DAERA website (https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/topics/grants-and-funding), which is updated as and when my Department launches funding schemes for applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the revised teachers' pay offer.
To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on any needed maintenance work to the roof of Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any support her Department can provide to promote Roe Valley Resort and golf course as a premier tourist and leisure destination.
To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the UK Government's proposed changes to the Personal Independence Payment.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to improve employment opportunities for people with autism.
answer_text: I recognise that many learners with special educational needs, including those who are autistic, face greater barriers at the point of leaving school and transitioning onto appropriate education, training and employment pathways. I am committed to taking action to address this and I expect to receive a report shortly from my officials who have been examining post-school provision for young people with special educational needs.   Additionally, my Department offers a range of skills provision which include measures to support individuals who face additional barriers, including autism, to enable them to build the skills and qualifications needed to compete for employment opportunities at all levels. Amongst these measures are Breaking Barriers in education, Skill Up and Step Up’s Disability Access Route to Employment and Education (DARE).  Additionally, targeted support for Widening Participation into higher education is provided through interventions and financial support for several target groups, including those with a disability (physical, sensory or learning) including Autism. My Department’s Careers Service is also working to introduce additional interactive technology which can help address the specific issues autistic people face when trying to access careers information and advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any policies her Department intends to introduce to support people with autism into the workplace.
answer_text: I recognise that many learners with special educational needs, including those who are autistic, face greater barriers at the point of leaving school and transitioning onto appropriate education, training and employment pathways.I am committed to taking action to address this issue and I expect to receive a report shortly from my officials who have been examining post-school provision for young people with special educational needs.  My Department’s Careers Service is also working to develop and introduce additional interactive technology which can address the specific issues those with autism face when trying to access careers information. In terms of the workplace, I consulted on proposals for a New Employment Rights Bill last year.  That consultation sought views on a range of proposals, some of which may be beneficial to people with autism in the workplace, such as flexible working.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the safer routes to school initiative.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to help address Northern Ireland having the highest disability employment gap in the UK.
answer_text: My department offers a range of practical, financial and other support to those with disabilities to enable them to develop skills and qualifications needed to compete for employment opportunities. This includes measures such as Breaking Barriers in education and Step Up. In addition, targeted support for Widening Participation into higher education is provided  for several target groups, including those with a disability. We also have a strong apprenticeship offering which provides additional support to those with a disability. I recognise that more can be done to increase participation from individuals with a disability.  The Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund will provide up to £600,000 to facilitate collaborative projects to derive innovative solutions, test new inclusion approaches and deliver key methodologies to tackle our inclusion challenges.  My Department has supported the Business In The Community Opening Doors Inclusive Recruitment Campaign.  This campaign is aimed at employers and will support them to extend their recruitment practices to reach more people who are economically inactive or face barriers to entering the labour market. One of my priorities is good jobs, this means ensuring that good quality work is accessible to everyone.  There are clear business benefits to widening the pool of potential employees and clear economic and social benefits to ensuring that no groups are locked out of good quality work. This campaign will provide vital support for businesses on this journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he intends to carry out a review of the Core Grant Funding Scheme.
answer_text: The Core Grant Scheme was reviewed by my officials and redesigned in partnership with the Community and Voluntary Sector in the months leading up to the launch of the 2025/26 Scheme in November 2024. Feedback and learning captured from the implementation of the new Scheme will be considered and where appropriate amendments will be made in advance of the launch of the 2026/27 Scheme later this year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the priorities that they wish to raise during any visit to the United States of America for St. Patrick’s Day.
answer_text: Our priorities for engaging internationally are focused on promoting NI as a stable and successful region and ensuring our international partners recognise how great a place this is to live, work, study and visit. These priorities will be highlighted during our visit to the US, and by Junior Ministers during their visit to Brussels.If we are to achieve our aim to grow a globally competitive sustainable economy that benefits all our citizens, we need to showcase our region as an attractive and dynamic investment location.  We need to build on the successes we have had, consolidate the support and partnerships we have internationally, and foster new relationships.                                Our international engagements give us a platform to show what we have delivered as an Executive over the past year; to show how far we have come; and build relationships with those who can help us deliver more in the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale why Givinostat is not available for children with Duchenne muscular dystrophy; and (ii) any action he will take to ensure that Givinostat is made available.
answer_text: I can confirm that the manufacturer has made givinostat available via an Early Access Programme (EAP) for eligible patients in parts of the UK.  Decisions relating to participation in EAPs in advance of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) decisions are for HSC Trusts to take.  The Belfast Trust, as the regional provider of specialist paediatric neuromuscular services, has advised that it is not in a position to facilitate the givinostat EAP due to the associated resource implications.My Department has a formal link with the NICE under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within HSC organisations.  This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.  In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.NICE is currently in the early stages of evaluating givinostat (Duvyzat®) for the treatment of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in people 6 years and over, with final guidance expected to publish in July 2025.  When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any legislation he plans to introduce in the near future.
answer_text: Since receiving this question I have brought to the assembly the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill.Any additional legislation or bills for introduction during the rest of the mandate cannot be introduced without the agreement of the Northern Ireland Executive. Further updates on legislative priorities will be communicated to the Northern Ireland Assembly as soon as they have been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive. A Written Ministerial Statement from the First Minister and deputy First Minister will issue in the coming weeks confirming the full legislative agenda for the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of the flu vaccine for people aged from 50 to 64 years old.
answer_text: I agreed to extend the annual influenza programme to include everyone aged 50 to 64 years of age on 7 January 2025 and by the 10 February 2025, I can advise that 91,881 individuals aged 50 to 64 years old had taken up the offer of the influenza vaccination. This equates to 24.56% of that age cohort. The seasonal influenza vaccination programme is a critical element in helping to protect the health of our population to reduce the pressures on our health and care services during the winter months. In early January the HSC was witnessing the impact of a more severe influenza season. Achieving a high uptake rate amongst all eligible groups, including health and social care staff, helps to reduce GP consultations, unplanned hospital admissions, pressure on Emergency Departments as well as staff absences due to flu related sickness. Extending the programme to all those aged 50 to 64 years of age will make use of the available purchased influenza vaccine stock. The quantities of influenza vaccine procured for the programme in Northern Ireland were informed by the advice from JCVI which determined the eligible cohort populations at the start of the programme. Vaccination remains the best form of defence against severe illness, hospitalisation and death as a result of influenza. The offer of influenza vaccination remains open until the end of March, and I would encourage anyone eligible, but particularly those aged 65 years or over, anyone with underlying health issues and Health and Social Care staff, to take up the offer as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any lessons learned from the winter 2024-25 pressures in hospital Emergency Departments; and (ii) any action he is taking to ensure the negative experiences of patients are not repeated in the 2025-26 winter season.
answer_text: Earlier this year, under the leadership of the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, I brought together senior leaders from across the Health and Social Care system to explore the learning from last winter, as we plan for winter 2025/26. I was clear that I wanted a whole system approach looking at what could be achieved both in advance of this winter and in the longer-term.This process identified the need for a particular focus on interventions to improve the care of those older people living with frailty and other co-morbidities, who have been identified as some of the most vulnerable to the risks associated with the winter period, and this will be reflected in this year’s plan. Seven action plans have been established which focus on reducing hospital demand by supporting older people to stay well and receive appropriate care in the community. This includes improving the use of advance clinical care planning and enhancing pathways for patients, ensuring the right care is delivered in the right place, first time.Of course, like in previous years, this year’s Winter Preparedness Plan will also identify other steps that my Department and key partners are taking to help manage pressures, including enhancements for General Practice, efforts to tackle ambulance handover delays and additions to Community Pharmacy services. We have also mobilised an extensive vaccination programme, to minimise the impact from winter infections such as influenza, RSV and COVID-19.I will publish the Winter Preparedness Plan imminently and, as in previous years, we will again call upon the public to do everything possible to help protect our system, including utilising community services, where available, and getting vaccinated, if eligible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of a footway on the Seacoast Road, Limavady, from Magilligan Presbyterian Church to St Aidan's Primary School.
answer_text: In November 2024, I approved the commissioning of six major Active Travel Signature projects across the North as part of my Department’s strategy for increasing investment in active travel to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022. One of the schemes under development is the Bellarena to Bishop’s Gate signature project which includes active travel plans for Magilligan and proposes linkages to key local and regional attractors such as Bellarena Train Station, Magilligan Community Centre, Presbyterian Church, St. Aidan’s Primary school, local sports clubs and others.  Widespread public and stakeholder engagement will be undertaken in due course at various stages of the design development process.Consultants are currently being appointed to commence scheme design and I expect to be in a position to make an announcement on this in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for the updated position on the proposed merger of Dunluce School, North Coast Integrated College and Coleraine College.
answer_text: The Education Authority is continuing to progress the technical feasibility report and business case for the ‘Day One’ project to establish the new school for September 2026. The feasibility report is expected to be completed by February 2025, with the business case submitted to the Department shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of (i) the current sustained media coverage of pressures within Emergency Departments; and (ii) how this will impact attracting and retaining nurses.
answer_text: (i) I am not aware of any empirical evidence of the impact of media coverage of pressures within Emergency Departments. However, it would be logical to assume it has resulted in some degree of chill factor, especially for those wondering whether they should self-refer to an ED. I also believe debates in the Assembly, such as the recent one on the Altnagelvin ED, have seen language used that would be potentially off-putting for those in need of ED services.(ii) It is not known if media coverage has any impact on those thinking of a career in nursing. However, having visited seven EDs since Christmas, I have spoken to nurses working across NI’s Eds and it is clear the relentless pressures the face puts a strain on our ability to retain them, particularly within Emergency Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the impact of his Department’s budget on carrying out repairs to the schools’ estate.
answer_text: The current capital budget constraints means that it is very challenging to effectively manage the schools’ estate and carry out all the repairs required across an estate of over 1,100 schools. There has been historic under-investment in the estate, and the Department is now faced with managing an ageing schools’ estate at a time of rising construction costs, alongside a requirement to provide large numbers of additional specialist places for rapidly increasing number of children with Special Educational Needs. Due to the budget constraints, decisions have necessarily been taken to direct available capital investment and maintenance budget to keep schools open, pupils safe and provide education access for the most vulnerable. I will continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained investment in our education system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund will provide investment for football at all levels. It is a key priority for me and I am committed to moving forward at pace.I want to roll out the funding as quickly as possible, however, it is important that funding is disbursed in a fair and transparent manner. The Northern Ireland Football Fund will prioritise performance clubs first, but I am also moving forward with proposals to invest at grassroots level and in creation of a National Football Centre.I approved the Business Case for the Performance Programme in December 2024 and in the coming weeks I expect to open a call for applications to the Fund from performance clubs.  Following the application and assessment process, I anticipate that the first group of clubs selected for support will be identified early in the new financial year. This will begin a rolling program of awards and project delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the removal of car parking availability at Ballycarton Forest, Limavady, contributes to his Department's strategy to promote greater access to green spaces.
answer_text: The area at Ballycarton Woods, previously a forest operational yard, has facilitated a local use for parking cars. However, this was not a formal carpark facility. Forest Service erected fencing at the former forest yard in Ballycarton Woods to limit the occurrence of anti-social vehicle use, following reports by residents, and concerns about public safety.  The information received was substantiated by Forest Service through engagement with the PSNI. The works undertaken recognise the need to accommodate a level of car parking space to meet local needs and to allow the continued and safe use of the access road by all users.Forest Service promote well-established and successful partnership arrangements, with many councils and other recreational partners, which has enabled significant development and investment in forest based recreational infrastructure, including the provision of improved access.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an assessment has been made on the impact that closing car parking availability at Ballycarton Forest, Limavady, has on the health benefits for forest walkers.
answer_text: Although it was necessary to carry out works to limit the scope for anti-social vehicle use and manage public safety, Forest Service continues to accommodate a level of car parking space to meet local user needs, including for forest walkers, at Ballycarton Woods.  The wider Binevenagh forest area, which encompasses Ballycarton Woods, also has carparking facilities adjacent to Binevenagh lake to accommodate visitors walking in the forest and hence help to provide health benefits to them.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to revise the number of children that can be cared for by a registered childminder.
answer_text: The Department developed and published the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12 in 2012 which supplement and support the legal requirements set out in The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and associated Guidance and Regulations (Volume 2).The standards provide families in Northern Ireland with assurance of a consistent level of quality in childcare services and ensure a regional approach to registration and inspection across HSC Trusts. The childcare landscape has however changed significantly since 2012, and I am fully aware of the range of challenges currently facing the sector. The delivery of affordable and accessible childcare is a commitment of the Northern Ireland Executive and effective legislative and standards frameworks will be a key to delivering on that commitment.Work is underway across Departments, led by the Department of Education, to support the development of a new Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy with the dual aims of supporting child development and enabling parental employment. I have made a commitment to undertake a full review of the Minimum Standards. While it is important that the Standards and Implementation Guidance are reviewed in their entirety, there are two issues which have been raised regularly with the Department. Firstly, the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration, countersigned by their GP, in advance of taking up employment; and secondly, the adult to child ratio requirements. It is my intention to consult publicly on these two key issues initially, followed by a review of the wider standards later in 2025.  A wide range of stakeholders have already expressed an interest in coming forward to assist with the review, and my officials will be contacting those individuals in the coming weeks to formally commence the review.

answer_text: The salted network is 7,000km long, covering 28% of the road network and facilitating approximately 80% of traffic.  Details of all salting routes can be viewed on my Department’s website via the following link; DFI Roads Salted Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the plans his Department has to regularly clean directional road signs; and (ii) the cleaning schedule currently in place.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example defective signs and signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.However, my Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities has been under significant pressure for a number of years as a result of a decade of tory austerity and cuts. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Defective, damaged or missing signs as well as those needing to be cleaned, are categorised as urgent or non-urgent work with priority given to mandatory and warning signs. Cleaning of other signs is carried out as permitted by availability of resources.
