question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the creation of a shadow board and Chief Executive Officer to deliver on a single regional children and families arms-length body.
answer_text: I am supportive of the recommendation within the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services to establish a Children and Families Arms-length Body.  The recommendation is for an ALB which includes current HSCT statutory children’s social care services along with other allied services and professions closely linked to children’s social care.  This includes youth justice services, youth services and education welfare services.  I consider that a step in this direction has to be capable of delivering additional benefits now and have the potential to evolve over time.  A new ALB has to be more than the drawing together of children’s social care from 5 Health and Social Care Trusts.   Given the significant and cross-cutting nature of this recommendation, I have asked Executive colleagues to consider the recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to provide defibrillator devices in all schools.
answer_text: This week I announced a significant new initiative to invest more than £700,000 of funding to purchase and install defibrillators to all schools in Northern Ireland.It is anticipated that the provision will be rolled out to all schools by the end of 2025, which will boost availability of defibrillators for all communities across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration she is giving to supporting local councils affected by the recently announced employer's National Insurance increases.
answer_text: Following the Autumn Budget announcement my Department was advised the Chancellor had decided to provide funding to the public sector to support it with the additional cost associated with the changes to employer National Insurance Contributions. Treasury officials have now advised that the Barnett formula will apply, in the usual way, to changes agreed to Whitehall departments’ budgets. It is hoped that the figures will be confirmed before Westminster Supplementary estimates which are usually early in the new year. While the Department does not have a confirmed estimate from Treasury it is unlikely to be sufficient to meet the increased costs. I have written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to stress the need for the cost impact to be met in full by the British Government. My officials will continue to engage with colleagues in the Department for Communities to understand the impact of the changes on local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether cardiovascular issues will be included in the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The Women’s Health Action Plan will consider a broad range of health issues faced by women throughout the life course.I met recently with the British Heart Foundation prior to the launch of their Bias and Biology Northern Ireland report regarding the Heart Attack Gender Gap. I welcome the report which highlights issues of under-awareness, under-diagnosis and under-treatment of cardiovascular disease among women, and provides a number of helpful policy recommendations which my Department will now consider.  I recognise that there is a need for improved education and information on various women’s health issues and risk factors, to support women and healthcare professionals in the identification, management and treatment of conditions including cardiovascular disease, which is one of the leading causes of death among women.  This will be considered as work on the action plan progresses. Officials have also met, and will continue to engage with, the Cardiac Network to consider what priority actions could be included in terms of improving outcomes for women in terms of their heart health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the £17 million allocation to his Department in the October Monitoring round for winter fuel support.
answer_text: I successfully bid and secured £17 million in the October Monitoring round to help mitigate the Winter Fuel Payment changes announced by the Labour Government. This will be used to issue a one-off winter fuel payment of £100 to all pensioner households in Northern Ireland affected by the Labour Governments’ decision. My officials are now working at pace to prepare and bring forward the necessary legislation and finalise the arrangements for making this emergency winter fuel payment.It is anticipated the payment will be made via existing payment channels before the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action she is taking to provide rates relief to independent retailers and high street businesses.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 17794/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of the Safer Routes to School initiative.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the review report with my officials.  I have asked for the report to be shared with the Infrastructure Committee, following which it will be made publicly available.My Department is also updating its active travel design guidance which will highlight best practice with respect to measures around schools to promote safety and encourage parents and pupils to walk, wheel or cycle to school where possible.I recently launched a public consultation on a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan.  Routes to schools are a key priority within the plan. I would encourage everyone to engage with this consultation to help shape priorities for the delivery of pedestrian and cycling infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Ballykelly Primary School will be included in a future roll-out of the Safer Routes to School initiative.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the review report with my officials. I have asked for the report to be shared with the Infrastructure Committee, following which it will be made publicly available.Meanwhile concerns about driver behaviour such as speeding or other inconsiderate behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their non-emergency 101 telephone number.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The purpose of the plan is to highlight the wide range of policy and service development initiatives currently in progress, and to outline the actions and improvements that can be delivered over the next three years to improve health outcomes for women. At this stage the focus is on mapping the work that is already under way across the health and social care system (including, for example improving maternity, gynae and menopause services, cervical screening and breast cancer services, and planning for a mother and baby unit) and identifying areas where further action is needed (for example, improving public health awareness around women’s health, providing more training and education for healthcare professionals on these issues, promoting healthy lifestyles and tackling health inequality). My Department will be running a number of engagement events from January to listen to women and further inform the development of the Action Plan.The pace and extent of delivery will be determined by our available capacity and resources, which remain under extreme pressure - this is well documented. With regard to the budget required – clearly what is desirable exceeds what is available for the foreseeable future. However, there are many initiatives under way which have received investment, or can be delivered at low cost or at no additional cost within current budgets (for example, in 2024 the introduction of primary HPV screening for cervical cancer, and the delivery of menopause training to many healthcare professionals).In light of this my focus will be on the actions that can be delivered within current resources to stabilise and improve services, and to tackle the health inequalities that contribute to morbidity and poor outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service’s Estate Strategy 2023-2030 Making the Place for Justice.
answer_text: Modernisation of the courts and tribunals estate is a key part of the Vision 2030 portfolio within NI Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS), and the publication of its Estate Strategy in December 2023 was an important milestone for the Agency. The Estate Strategy is structured around four Aims to improve User Experience, Resilience, Value for Money and Sustainability. Realising these Aims will result in a courts and tribunals estate that is capable of providing modernised and improved facilities for all citizens of Northern Ireland.The Estate Strategy provides a long-term framework for evidence-based investment over the next decade and beyond. In terms of implementation, the 2024/25 delivery plan included twenty specific actions, with more than half of these already completed and the rest ongoing.These include commencing procurement for the Modernisation of Bishop Street Courthouse project, and continuing the next stages in the designs for the Modernisation of the Royal Courts of Justice project. Subject to funding and business case approval, these two major capital construction projects will modernise, decarbonise and secure the future of two Grade A listed buildings in Londonderry and Belfast.  During the development of the Estate Strategy, NICTS established an Estate Strategy Advisory Group (ESAG), comprising senior stakeholders, partners and user group representatives. This group will continue to meet as the Strategy is implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total compensation payments paid to patients; and (ii) the total legal costs incurred as a result in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of health and social care staff disciplined in relation to patient safety breaches in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information about HSC employers’ disciplinary actions on their staff. This information is the responsibility of the HSC Trusts as the employers of our HSC workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the cross departmental work to legislate for Post-19 Special Educational Needs provision.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) why the October initiative for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery at the Royal Hospital for Sick Children has now ceased; and (ii) when and where percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery will recommence.
answer_text: The Department of health can confirm that percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery activity has not ceased at the Royal Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC).  My officials have been working with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) to identify measures in order to try and further address waiting times for this procedure.  The Belfast Trust has commenced additional clinics and theatre sessions to help reduce paediatric waiting times and additional Paediatric Gastrostomy procedures have been approved through waiting list initiative funding.  The Trust has now scheduled additional weekend theatre sessions dates between October and December to help address the backlog.  A Paediatric Surgeon has now completed a validation of Peg Tube waiting lists to identify which children would be clinically suitable to transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  The clinical criteria for transfer has been agreed by the specialist team to determine this. BHSCT is currently in negotiations with Alder Hey Hospital to identify what capacity they may have to offer. However, like RBHSC, they are currently also experiencing unscheduled seasonal pressures. The capacity required will be pre-assessment of these complex children, theatre capacity, inpatient bed capacity (children stay between 48-72 hours), parental training to manage the peg tube, equipment and food, and a follow up appointment. Further discussions are also underway with South Eastern Trust to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon with a surgical list in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this. A number of children have been identified to be managed on the South Eastern Trust theatre list.A further review of surgical work performed in RBHSC is underway and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining partnership working with the Private Sector however there are a number of areas that the Trust would need to have assurance on for the delivery of safe and effective care of these complex children many of which have other multiple co-morbidities.My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions and examine their progress and success.I want to assure you that officials in the Department as well as colleagues in BHSCT are continuing to work hard to try and address this issue but, given the ongoing pressures on the service and demand for theatre capacity and beds, this will mean that this will not be solved immediately.  We will continue to assess and gauge progress on this to ensure that all of the children and young people receive the care they require as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to (i) stabilise children’s social work services; (ii) improve working conditions for staff, including safe staffing levels; and (iii) address critical shortages in frontline social work, particularly in the (a) Gateway Service teams; and (b) Family Support and Intervention teams.
answer_text: My Department has worked collaboratively with HSC Trusts, Universities and Colleges, trade union representatives and other key stakeholders to alleviate some of the pressures brought about as a result of recruitment and retention issues in HSC social work teams. A Social Work Workforce Review was undertaken in 2021 and its findings were published in 2022. The Department then established the Social Work Workforce Implementation Board, involving senior leaders and a range of workforce representatives, to plan and deliver real improvements in the short, medium and longer term. A key objective which has emerged since the publication of the Workforce Review has been to produce safe staffing guidance for HSC social work services. Developmental work is well underway and it is hoped that guidance will become available in 2025.Considerable progress has also been made in alleviating pressures and improving stability in the social work workforce, including:in December 2022, the decision was taken to end the use of recruitment agency social workers in all HSC services by June 2023.the Trusts then recruited around 160 agency social workers into vacant HSC posts between January and June 2023 and all usage of recruitment agency social workers in HSC has now ceased. the Department created a faster, more efficient route into Trust employment for newly qualified social workers, which resulted in 208 vacancies being filled between June and September 2023. so far in 2024, a further 206 newly qualified social workers have begun working in HSC Trusts. The majority of vacancies which have been filled in both 2023 and 2024 have been in Children’s Services.the Department has also increased the number of training places for student social workers by commissioning 25 places with the Open University NI between 2021-2023 and has added an additional 40 training places in 2024. This brings the total number of social work training places to 325, the largest number on record.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he intends to introduce the dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: It is my intention to introduce a Dilapidation Bill to the Assembly in the autumn term of this current sitting, subject of course to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. The proposed Bill would provide councils with a modern and fit-for-purpose regime, including a clear toolkit of powers to tackle dilapidated buildings and neglected sites, including a series of notices, offences, penalties and appeals. Powers would be similar to those available to local authorities in other parts of the UK where low level dilapidation can be dealt with at a much earlier stage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that orthopaedic hubs are to assist with reducing waiting times, (i) whether their remit is to focus on less complex day case surgeries; and (ii) whether patients with more complex needs, such as those with comorbidities or who are older, will have longer treatment waits.
answer_text: The remit of the 3 elective orthopaedic hubs in Northern Ireland is to focus on surgeries of all levels of complexity for both inpatient and daycase procedures.  The elective orthopaedic hubs aim to reduce waiting times for all patients, regardless of comorbidities or age.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what discussions she has had with the PSNI regarding the future of Limavady Police Station.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his Department's estimated cost of a bypass around Ballykelly village.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the actions his Department is taking to lessen the impact for road users affected by the A2 Clooney Road Active Travel Scheme.
answer_text: Road works by their nature can be disruptive but officials in my department are working with the contractor to ensure that work is carried out as efficiently as possible. Two-way traffic is maintained where possible and lane closures are only used when necessary for the safety of scheme operatives. To mitigate the impact on the travel in the arear, the contractor’s traffic management permit includes working restrictions during the morning and evening peak hours and night-time working is scheduled between 14 October and 30 October. All these measures are aimed at reducing disruption to the travelling public whilst implementing this important active travel scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his Department's updated position on the delivery of a bypass of Ballykelly village.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.My Department is in the process of developing a new Transport Strategy that will help to shape the vision and strategic priorities for transport through to 2035.  Once this is published, it will help to inform the Transport Plan for the Regional Strategic Transport Network, which includes the A2 at Ballykelly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of the concerns of the British Medical Association in relation to how the GP Indemnity is being funded.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to address dog attacks on family pets.
answer_text: Dog owners are responsible for controlling their dog and can be prosecuted under the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (the Order) if their dog attacks and injures an animal owned by another person. Councils enforce dog control legislation in Northern Ireland and the Order provides significant powers for council officers to investigate and take legal action in relation to all incidents of dog attacks. My Department promotes responsible dog ownership on an ongoing basis and has provided a range of information on NI Direct about all aspects of dog ownership including the need to control a dog in public.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to (i) improve the diagnosis; and (ii) raise awareness of Alzheimer's disease.
answer_text: (i) The Department recognises the urgency with which individuals awaiting assessment will wish to be seen in order to understand their condition and to commence treatment.  The Department has issued a 9-week target which sets out that people should have a first assessment following referral into Health and Social Care Trust memory services within 9 weeks. All Trusts have advised that they employ a rigorous triaging regime on receipt of dementia referrals to ensure that those individuals at greatest risk are seen first. My officials continue to engage with those HSC Trusts where there is underperformance against this 9-week target. A level of underperformance is attributable to workforce gaps within Psychiatry of Old Age. However, Trusts are continuing with service improvement initiatives and recruitment exercises. (ii) Our Health and Social Care Trusts, and their community partners, are committed to supporting and increasing awareness and resources for those living with dementia, and those who care for them.   Each Trust hosts, facilitates and supports a number of Dementia Awareness campaigns and initiatives throughout the year, these include the use of the Virtual Dementia Bus, promotion of Dementia awareness at memory walks and local marathons etc. Each Trust also employs staff who are specially trained in relation to Dementia.  These include Dementia Navigators who provide support and advice to people when they are diagnosed with dementia and provide a point of contact within each HSC Trust throughout the person`s ‘Living with Dementia’ Journey.  The Dementia Navigator holds a remit of health promotion, safer community living and living well with Dementia.  Dementia Friendly Partnerships have also been formed across communities in Northern Ireland, they bring together local businesses and public facing services and facilitate Dementia Awareness training to their staff.Partnership working includes work with the Alzheimer’s Society and Dementia NI who provide training to HSC staff, the general public, local businesses and public facing services, which has included Translink and major supermarket chains.  These groups also provide support and advocacy for the person living with Dementia and for their carers.  My department has established a Regional Dementia Project Board to refocus on Dementia Care as a priority across Northern Ireland.  The need for a renewed focus on Dementia Diagnosis and on Dementia Education and Training, across Northern Ireland, has been recognised as a priority.  The Project Board has established two workstreams to take these areas of work forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of FibroScan machines available for use in Northern Ireland, broken down by location.
answer_text: As at September 2024, Belfast Trust had two fibroscan machines operating as part of the Hepatology Service in the Trust. The other four Trusts have one machine each.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures in place in each Health and Social Care Trust to provide for the early detection of liver disease.
answer_text: The current pathway for people identified in primary care with suspected liver disease is referral to secondary care services in their local Trust. Some initial discussions have taken place between colleagues in the Belfast Trust Hepatology Service and local primary care representatives around refinement to the local pathway for services provided in Belfast Trust, specifically around non-alcohol fatty liver disease (NAFLD).A pilot pathway has already been trialled with one GP practice.  This involved:A GP identifying a patient with NAFLD and undertaking initial screening and then sending a referral to the Hepatology ServiceThe referral is triaged by a consultant and is either forwarded for a consultant clinical appointment or if appropriate to a nurse led clinicIf the appointment is triaged to a nurse led clinic a fibroscan is undertaken and the nurse can provide lifestyle advice regarding NAFLDIf there are any abnormal findings on the scan then the patient is referred on to a consultant clinic, if not then they are discharged back to their GP with appropriate advice.Due to the success of this pilot the Regional Liver Unit is now applying the criteria to NAFLD referrals from all Belfast Trust practices and is also exploring the implications for similar pathway arrangements in other Trust areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether blue-green algae blooms are present at Ballinrees Reservoir at Windyhill Road, Coleraine; and (ii) how widespread are these blooms.
answer_text: The issue you have raised with regards Ballinrees Reservoir is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that algae blooms are present, to a greater or lesser extent at most raw water sources at this time. Blue Green / Cyanobacteria are often, but not always, present within an algae bloom. The current blue green/ Cyanobacteria count at Ballinrees reservoir is 2000 with the total algae count 5000. Should you have any further queries with regards this you can contact NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: My Department has carried out an Open Market Review and public consultation for Project Gigabit to determine the current and planned availability of gigabit-capable broadband availability here, and has conducted Pre-Procurement Market Engagement with interested suppliers in expectation of carrying out a formal procurement exercise for the project later this year. The Open Market Review identified 59,000 premises which are eligible for intervention under Project Gigabit, and a further 60,000 premises where suppliers have reported current coverage which has not been verified, or planned build where there is risk to delivery. However, to ensure balance between providing room for commercial investment and the objective of ensuring that homes and businesses can access gigabit-capable infrastructure as soon as possible, and in accordance with Subsidy Control Classification Guidance published by Building Digital UK, not all eligible premises will automatically be included in the initial scope of a procurement under Project Gigabit. My Department and BDUK have continued to assess market investment plans since the Open Market Review, and intervention will be deferred to some eligible premises which may be addressed commercially. Intervention may also be deferred for eligible premises which are adjacent to premises where gigabit-capable infrastructure exists, or there are firm plans to build over the next three years. Eligible premises which have been treated as deferred can be added to the scope of a contract if future market reviews show no credible commercial plans. The number of premises being considered for Initial Scope of an awarded contract will be confirmed in the coming weeks with the initial phase of procurement, subject to final approvals.  My Department expects to award a contract for Project Gigabit by March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any new treatments available to patients who have received a dementia diagnosis.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system in Northern Ireland, with the region projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040. My Department recognises the importance of fostering an ethos of ongoing improvement on all issues pertaining to dementia services across Northern Ireland.  The HSC Trusts provide Dementia assessment and management using a multi-disciplinary model involving staff such as doctors, nurses, psychologists, social workers, occupational and speech and language therapists and peer and voluntary support workers.   While management of Dementia is multi-disciplinary and not solely dependent on medication, Northern Ireland was one of the first regions to make dementia medications available to citizens.  There is free prescribing of the currently available drugs.In relation to the new treatments for dementia, specifically Lecanemab. Whilst the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) approved a product licence for Lecanemab, it is not recommended by NICE (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence).   HSC Trusts are unable to recommend treatments to patients which have not been approved by NICE.  Patients with a confirmed diagnosis of Alzheimer’s disease will continue to access drug treatments which are currently licensed and approved. My Department has recently established a Regional Dementia Project Board to bring a sharpened focus on improvement in dementia care and services across the region.  One of the key areas of focus for this group will be to consider our ‘system readiness’ as the introduction of such treatments will bring a need for additional diagnostic resources and potential changes to the workforce across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will carry out a workforce review into diagnostic and interventional radiology.
answer_text: My Department is aware that the imaging workforce in Northern Ireland continues to be constrained by insufficient numbers. A costed workforce plan for imaging services, including diagnostic and interventional radiology, has been developed and outlines requirements to bring training within imaging teams up to date of approximately £6m per annum, over the next 3-5 years. Currently this remains unfunded.However, a business case is being developed for a multi-professional imaging training academy for Northern Ireland which will address radiology and advanced practice radiographer workforce deficit. The Imaging Academy intends to deliver an overall increase in imaging capacity within Health and Social Care for the benefit of all clinical specialties and support the reduction of excess waiting lists for diagnosis and treatment, as well as meeting the demands from unscheduled care.Progress remains subject to business case approval and availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to expanding clinical radiology speciality training places.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions his Department has had with the Royal College of Radiologists in relation to improving recruitment into clinical oncology and to ensure all training places are filled.
answer_text: On 29 July 2024 my officials and I met the President of the Royal College of Radiologists, Dr Katherine Halliday, to consider the findings of their 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.The College outlined the initiatives it had undertaken to encourage Clinical Oncology recruitment.  It was agreed that the College would engage with the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency to support the ongoing process of recruitment into locally available clinical oncology training places.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline her discussions with the new British Government regarding the need for additional funding.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he plans to engage with the animal welfare sector in relation to the drafting of legislation to introduce a ban on the third-party sale of puppies and kittens.
answer_text: I am keen to engage with the animal welfare sector and I intend to facilitate discussions with key stakeholders to assist me in appropriately regulating third-party sales of puppies and kittens in Northern Ireland. Any proposals I am minded to introduce, in due course, will also be subject to public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of general surgery in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Trust is continuing to develop plans for the delivery of general surgical services across its hospital sites in the context of the Review of General Surgery. The Trust aims to shortly launch a public consultation and a report should be with the Department by late 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what action their Department is taking to address the exploitation of children by paramilitaries.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the projections in the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Clinical Oncology Workforce Census that the shortfall of clinical oncologists could rise to 12 per cent by 2028.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the workforce challenges that exists across health and social care and therefore welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.  I have already agreed to meet representatives from the Royal College to discuss the findings of these reports in detail, including their predictions of shortfalls in the current and future number of clinical oncologists and clinical/interventional radiologists.My Department is committed to the provision of effective imaging and cancer services in Northern Ireland, ensuring patient safety and quality of care.  The Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care (2018) recognised that our existing training mechanism could not deliver the number of clinical radiologists required to meet increasing demand.  While much has been achieved over recent years, with training places having been increased by 46% from 37 (2015/16) to 54 (2022/23), the Department recognises that a more innovative approach is required to generate the radiologist workforce required to meet future demand.After extensive consideration of best practice options, including those set out in the Royal College of Radiology’s Radiology Training 2016 – 2026: A vision and a solution, the Department and HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland.  This will significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.Following a robust options appraisal process by an independent expert panel, the Mater Infirmorum Hospital site in Belfast HSC Trust has been identified as the optimum location for the proposed Imaging Academy.  This would be expected to provide trainees with the additional clinical and office space and full Picture Archiving and Community System access recommended by the Royal College of Radiologists.  However, progressing this initiative remains subject to business case approval and the availability of funding (£3.3m is currently earmarked for establishment of the Imaging Academy in the Department’s draft Capital Plan).Pending the establishment of the Imaging Academy, a costed workforce plan (approximately £6m per annum over the next 3-5 years) for imaging services has also been developed, to bring training within imaging teams, including clinical radiologists, up to date.  Regrettably, funding for this programme remains unapproved.Regarding interventional radiology, the Regional Imaging Board has endorsed a Hub and Spoke model for this service.  This will utilise existing resources across the region, optimising specialist care provision to high standards and improving access for patients.  The model exists in Belfast HSC Trust and has been extended to the Western HSC Trust.  However, there is no dedicated funding to roll this model

The Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care (2018) recognised that our existing training mechanism could not deliver the number of clinical radiologists required to meet increasing demand.  While much has been achieved over recent years, with training places having been increased by 46% from 37 (2015/16) to 54 (2022/23), the Department recognises that a more innovative approach is required to generate the radiologist workforce required to meet future demand.After extensive consideration of best practice options, including those set out in the Royal College of Radiology’s Radiology Training 2016 – 2026: A vision and a solution, the Department and HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland.  This will significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.Following a robust options appraisal process by an independent expert panel, the Mater Infirmorum Hospital site in Belfast HSC Trust has been identified as the optimum location for the proposed Imaging Academy.  This would be expected to provide trainees with the additional clinical and office space and full Picture Archiving and Community System access recommended by the Royal College of Radiologists.  However, progressing this initiative remains subject to business case approval and the availability of funding (£3.3m is currently earmarked for establishment of the Imaging Academy in the Department’s draft Capital Plan).Pending the establishment of the Imaging Academy, a costed workforce plan (approximately £6m per annum over the next 3-5 years) for imaging services has also been developed, to bring training within imaging teams, including clinical radiologists, up to date.  Regrettably, funding for this programme remains unapproved.Regarding interventional radiology, the Regional Imaging Board has endorsed a Hub and Spoke model for this service.  This will utilise existing resources across the region, optimising specialist care provision to high standards and improving access for patients.  The model exists in Belfast HSC Trust and has been extended to the Western HSC Trust.  However, there is no dedicated funding to roll this model

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of littering pollution at recreation areas, including beaches, along the North Coast.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions being taken to restart the paediatric gastroenterology service at the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The paediatric gastroenterology service recently experienced a loss of capacity due to a combination of planned and unplanned leave in the consultant team.The immediate priority was to maintain 24/7 access to specialist clinical advice for emergency and urgent patients together with an agreed pathway for transfer if required to other tertiary centres in Dublin and GB.Actions to secure specialist locum cover were ongoing throughout together with negotiations to secure in reach support for further resilience in the immediate period together with forward planning to address any backlog arising from suspension of planned elective work including outpatient appointments.  The service has now resumed to the full consultant team and the Trust are working to address the backlog in elective work in partnership with the support of an NHS GB provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to a media report on 29 May 2024, to detail the circumstances which resulted in parents being incorrectly informed of a referral for their child to specialists at Great Ormond Street Hospital for paediatric gastroenterology services.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust are aware of this issue and have been in communication with the family.  The Trust have confirmed that the family were advised of the potential for a referral to Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH)but that this would be informed by continued monitoring of the child’s condition and the outcome of a number of clinical investigations.  The child’s condition subsequently stabilised and remained under clinical review with the potential for referral to GOSH if clinically indicated.  Unfortunately, this small clinical team experienced a period of unplanned leave and the priority for the service was to maintain capacity for urgent and emergency care in the first instance, which impacted upon outpatient capacity including the review appointment scheduled for this child.Issues with capacity in the service have now been resolved and the Trust is actively engaged in securing the review of notes and test results for this child and hope to be in a position to advise the family of the outcome within the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the discussions he has had with his counterparts in England to consider a change to the residency requirement for social work students who study there.
answer_text: My Department has recently received the report ‘Campaign to Increase the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme, The Case for Change’ and is considering its findings.I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking professional training courses due to the cost-of-living crisis. Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation which I opposed but a majority of others MLA’S supported, the Department is currently unable to provide more assistance to students than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the Migraine Trust report entitled, The Impact of Migraine on People in Northern Ireland and Opportunities to Improve Care; and (ii) its recommendations for primary and specialist care for headache and migraine.
answer_text: I recognise the challenges within Neurology Services highlighted by the Migraine Trust Report ‘The impact of Migraine on People in Northern Ireland and Opportunities to Improve Care’. The Findings of this Report have been considered within the context of the recommendations arising from my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services. The Neurology Review has identified a range of recommendations to improve services across primary and secondary care. These include the introduction of referral management services to assist with the triage of outpatient referrals; and the provision of timely advice and guidance to GPs to assist with the effective management of neurology patients in primary care where appropriate. In addition, consideration has also been given to the development of extended roles such as GPs with Enhanced Roles and Advanced Nurse Practitioners to support the management of people with neurological conditions, including headache and migraine. It is anticipated that the Final Report of the Neurology Review will be completed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to (i) raise awareness among employers about the impact that migraine has on employees who live with this condition; and (ii) promote reasonable adjustment for migraine.
answer_text: The Department for Communities is committed to supporting people with disabilities and health conditions, including those who suffer from migraine, progress towards, move into and stay in employment. Work Coaches operate in each of the Department’s 35 Jobs & Benefits Offices.  They have a key purpose to work with customers to support them into work.  This includes addressing any barriers a customer might have that make it harder for them to enter and stay in employment.  Each Jobs & Benefits office also has developed relationships with local providers including those supporting individuals with health conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any estimates have been made of the potential for savings to the health system from more headache and migraine cases being handled in primary care.
answer_text: My Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services recognises the need to make the most of current resources. The Review has considered how patients are best supported; who provides that support; and whether it is best delivered in secondary care, primary care or in the community. In this context, the Review has identified a range of recommendations to generate efficiencies within the Neurology service through new and innovative ways of working. These include the introduction of referral management services to assist with the triage of outpatient referrals; and the provision of timely advice and guidance to GPs to assist with the effective management of neurology patients in primary care where appropriate. In addition, consideration has been given to the development of GPs with Enhanced Roles to build on the contribution of GPs within Neurology. Implementation of these recommendations will ensure that all people with a neurological condition, including those with headache and migraine, receive the right care in the right place at the right time. It is anticipated that the Final Report of the Neurology Review will be completed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) what policies are in place in universities to deal with sectarian behavior; and (ii) how these policies are enforced.
answer_text: The universities are autonomous institutions responsible for their own policies and procedures including those which relate to equality and discrimination issues. The Universities have a number of strategies and policies in place to combat sectarianism. Breaches of their various harassment and equality policies are enforced through their disciplinary procedures.Further details regarding each institution’s policies and procedures relating to equality, good relations, bullying and harassment and discipline can be found on their respective websites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans there are to establish an independent regulator for higher education.
answer_text: There are no current plans to establish an independent regulator for higher education. My Department will continue to perform regulatory functions to ensure that institutions deliver high quality education and research, remain financially viable and protect the interests of students.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the action his Department has taken to make school uniforms more affordable.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward legislation to the Assembly to make the guidance to schools about their uniform policy statutory.  The key driver underpinning this action is to ensure that school uniforms are more affordable for parents.  In advance of this, my Department will be issuing a consultation shortly on the areas to be covered in such statutory guidance.  The outcomes of this will enable the legislation and the associated guidance to both respect the rights of schools to ensure uniform requirements adequately reflect the ethos of their school, and also take sufficient account of the issue of affordability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the May 2024 report, Campaign to Increase the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme, the Case for Change; and (ii) the recommendation for an uplift in the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme to £7,000 per year.
answer_text: My Department has recently received the report ‘Campaign to Increase the Social Work Student Incentive Scheme, The Case for Change’ and is considering its findings.I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking professional training courses due to the cost-of-living crisis. Regrettably, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation following the passage of the 2024/25 budget, the Department is currently unable to provide more assistance to students than it does at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the 5 per cent consolidated pay uplift and £1505 non-consolidated payment for Health and Social Care Agenda for Change staff; and (ii) when these will be paid.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 10947/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to carry out an assessment for the provision of a traffic light system at the Strand Road roundabout, Coleraine, similar to the traffic light system at the Lodge Road roundabout.
answer_text: A full traffic study of the road network surrounding the Riverside Retail Park in Coleraine was carried out by my department in 2019. At the Strand Road Roundabout the maximum delay experienced was 5 minutes on Castleroe Road with the majority of the other approaches experiencing delays of less than a minute. These delays are not considered to be excessive, nor unique, as most strategic traffic routes experience some congestion at peak times, particularly where the main road, which carries larger volumes of traffic, has priority over the lower trafficked minor roads. Consequently, my department has no proposals to provide traffic signals at this location at this time, however, the roundabout will continue to be monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the installation of an MRI scanner at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed that preparation work for the installation of an MRI scanner at Causeway Hospital is ongoing and it is anticipated the scanner will be operational by February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether feedback is collated from parents who have had to use the interim pathology service in Alder Hey, Liverpool.
answer_text: The PHA and SPPG gave an undertaking to stakeholders (which includes parents that used the service) involved in planning of the interim move to Alder Hey to evaluate the service after 1 year. In addition to qualitative measures, it was agreed that an understanding of the experience of staff and most importantly, families, would be undertaken by an organisation external to Health and Social Care.  In light of the specialist and sensitive nature of this evaluation, Queens University Belfast were approached to undertake this work.  The evaluation project was supported by SPPG and PHA with advice being provided by a stakeholder representative steering group.  Unfortunately due to Covid pressures this evaluation was paused for a period of time. A full evaluation report has now been developed jointly by SPPG and PHA which encompasses the qualitative report produced by Queens University Belfast along with a full-service evaluation and includes key performance indictor information.The key findings from this report will be shared with stakeholders at an event in June before being published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the issues raised by the Royal College of Nursing during their evidence session with the Committee for Health on 18 April 2024.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, subject to cost limitations, whether he will consider carrying out construction works to bypass Ballykelly village in separate phases.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.The preferred route for Ballykelly Bypass was announced in 2010. The proposed scheme is for the construction of 4.2km of dual carriageway that will pass to the south of Ballykelly village, with a proposed junction at the Loughermore Road, providing access to and from the dual carriageway.The proposed bypass of Ballykelly could not become operational until it is fully complete, therefore constructing the bypass in separate phases would not be feasible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated costs to provide a bypass for Ballykelly village.
answer_text: The preferred route for Ballykelly Bypass was announced in 2010, however due to the lack of funding this scheme has not been progressed. The estimated cost to provide the bypass at that time was in the range of £35m-£40m, however given the passage of time this will have significantly increased due to recent inflationary pressures.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he was consulted in relation his Department's decision to remove the weekday local enhanced GP out-of-hours service at Scroggy Road Clinic, Limavady.
answer_text: There was no requirement to consult with me in relation to the Department’s decision to remove the weekday local enhanced GP out-of-hours service at Scroggy Road Clinic, Limavady. This is an operational issue.This is a Local Enhanced Service (LES) commissioned by the Directorate of Primary Care, Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital programme for Special Educational Needs provision.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many four bedroom social housing properties are currently planned to be constructed in East Londonderry; and (ii) where they will be located.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In the East Londonderry Constituency, there are currently 7 four-bedroom social housing units under construction, details of locations are in Table 1 below. An additional 19 four-bedroom social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2024/25 – 2026/27. Details of locations are provided in Table 2 below”.Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.Table 1: Four bedroom Social housing units under construction in the East Londonderry Parliamentary ConstituencyHousing AssociationScheme NameLocation TypeOnsite YearOnsite DateCompletionYear4-Bed House4-Bed BungalowChoiceThe Pines, Somerset RoadColeraineCompetitive Land Design & Build2021/2231/03/20222024/256 ChoiceThe Pines, Somerset RoadColeraineCompetitive Land Design & Build2021/2231/03/20222024/25 1Table 2: Four Bedroom Social housing units programmed to start through the SHDP 2024/25 – 2026/27 in the East Londonderry Parliamentary ConstituencyHousing AssociationScheme NameLocation TypeOnsite YearCompletionYear4-Bed HouseRadiusMillburn RoadColeraineCompetitive Land Design & Build2024/252025/262RadiusLaurel Hill, Coleraine Ph 3 (S)ColeraineNew Build2024/252025/267RadiusLaurel Hill, Coleraine Ph 3 (S)ColeraineNew Build2024/252025/262RadiusMountsandel RoadColeraineNew Build2024/252025/261RadiusMountsandel RoadColeraineNew Build2024/252025/261TriangleEdenmore RoadLimavadyNew Build2024/252026/271ClanmilColeraine RoadPortrushNew Build2025/262026/275”The delivery of these and the wider Social Housing Development Programme will be subject to sufficient capital budget being made available to my Department.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the report by the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health, Worried and Waiting: A review of paediatric waiting times in Northern Ireland, whether he will commit to an action plan to address the 172 per cent increase between June 2016 and June 2023 in children waiting for a first outpatient appointment.
answer_text: Work is already ongoing to address the waiting lists for children. However, it should be noted that despite the increasing waiting lists, Trusts are working very hard to see and treat large numbers of children and I would like to commend staff for their efforts. Despite the high number of children attending appointments, the number being referred for secondary care services is increasing exponentially, and we therefore need to understand the cause of that rise in referrals.Before the publication of the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health (RCPCH) Worried and Waiting Report, I had a very productive meeting with representatives of the RCPCH to discuss the report and its recommendations in detail and listen to suggestions of how to improve services.In addition to considering the issues raised by the RCPCH, the Child Health Partnership Programme Board is working hard to rebuild and reconfigure services following the pandemic – we will work closely with the RCPCH on this.I have already said that tackling waiting lists is a priority, but the scale of the waiting list crisis demonstrates the need for multi-year funding.  However, I am also keen to ensure that steps are taken within current resources to manage demand to ensure that children and young people are seen in a timely manner in the right settings appropriate to their individual needs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recruitment of a paediatric pathologist.
answer_text: SPPG and PHA remain committed to providing access to a high-quality perinatal pathology service. Significant efforts have been made to try and retain a service within Northern Ireland. However, the national and international shortage in paediatric pathologists is fully recognised. Due attention is also being paid to the reforms currently being made to NHS pathology services in England.The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) had undertaken an open-ended recruitment process, including with an agency specialising in international recruitment, but no suitably qualified candidate had been identified.Belfast Trust are currently in the process of reviewing and updating the Job Description to ensure it remains relevant and, after approval from Royal College of Pathology, will reissue this.Meanwhile, the service continues to be provided via a Service Level Agreement with Alder Hey Children’s Hospital Trust.  This is reviewed at regular intervals by my officials, Public Health Agency colleagues and Trust colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of comments by Dr Alan Stout at the Committee for Health meeting on 11 April 2024 that General Practice will collapse in the next 18 months if the current funding levels continue.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs and practice teams.  I am committed to ensuring the stability and longer-term sustainability of General Practice.  My Department has taken a number of steps to underpin the capacity of primary care and support the sustainability of General Practice and the GP workforce. In my previous term as Minister, I increased the number of GP training places to an all-time high of 121. This increase was maintained in the 2023/24 year.  Schemes are also in place to support the recruitment and retention of GPs, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme.	I want to continue to support General Practice by increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care.  My Department remains committed to the further implementation of the Multi-Disciplinary Team model across Northern Ireland and is developing a plan for rollout over the next number of years, subject to the availability of the necessary recurrent funding. 	Notwithstanding financial pressures, my Department has provided significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services. 	In October 2023, as part of the Department’s Winter Pressures Plan, £3.4million in funding was provided to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services to support the capacity of GP practices to meet the anticipated increase in demand over the winter period.  My Department also made £4.3million funding available to assist GP practices to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes this winter. In addition, £2.7million of GMS funding was reallocated to boost capacity.   		On returning as Health Minister, I moved quickly to approve the Doctor’s and Dentists’ Review Body (DDRB) recommendation of a 6% uplift for 2023/24 for independent General Medical Practitioners.  In addition, I also approved an uplift of 6% for staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift (3% recurrent) in other expenses.  	My Department has continued to boost the capacity of primary care by investing £25million per year in the Primary Care MDT Programme; £19million annually in the General Practice Pharmacy Programme, and £3.1million to recruit Advanced Nurse Practitioners.	In other steps to support the sustainability of General Practice, both in the short and medium term, my Department has been engaging with HSC Trusts and GP representatives to facilitate earlier involvement of Trusts in instances where GP practices are experiencing difficulty. Other work has also been progressed to develop guidance that will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care.  		My officials have also taken forward work with relevant NICS Departments and public bodies to explore ways to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices of completing non-statutory administrative tasks and the unn

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what autism training is provided to allied health professionals working in health and social care.
answer_text: Autism is a spectrum condition and affects people in different ways, and all our Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) offer training and support to all staff in the effective support of those patients with communication challenges and barriers, including autism, when accessing services, whilst also supporting staff to be more aware. HSCTs do take a service-wide approach to addressing the needs of those with Autism. This includes training for all staff, either run by individual Trusts, or commissioned from external organisations, as well as self-directed learning for those with a particular identified training need.Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) across our HSCTs have access to a full range of autism training including training in both diagnostic tools alongside intervention approaches. This can include in-service and professional training in relation to Autism as part of their continued professional development (CPD).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many GP practices have (i) handed back their contract in 2024; and (ii) indicated they will hand back their contract in 2024.
answer_text: When a GP Practice hands back their contract to provide GMS services, they must give a period of notice.  The length of the period of notice is determined by the status of the GP Practice – single handed GPs are required to give 3 months’ notice (from the end of the month in which they advise SPPG), GP Partnerships are required to give 6 months’ notice (from the end of the month in which they advise SPPG).During the 2024 calendar year so far, 5 GP Practices handed their contract back to SPPG and began the ‘notice period’ of either 3 or 6 months – these contracts will cease within the 2024 calendar year.  1 further GP Practice handed back their contract during the 2023 year and are still working through their ‘notice period’ – contract to cease within the 2024 calendar year.In addition, 1 GP Practice who handed back their contract in 2023 have finished their ‘notice period’ and the contract has now been taken over by the local Health and Social Care Trust in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a GP Indemnity Scheme for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally and I am committed to identifying solutions to address this issue for Northern Ireland GPs.  Recognising this, my Department recently reallocated funding from other areas of the GP budget to boost capacity in the service and mitigate demand and capacity pressures; this funding included support for additional costs of indemnity to enhance the capacity of the system to manage demand and was on a non recurrent basis. My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland. A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model to state intervention that includes a number of state backed model options. Departmental officials are continuing to take forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many GP surgeries in East Londonderry have a multi-disciplinary team.
answer_text: The MDT model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.All 17 practices within East Londonderry have MDT staff in post. A breakdown by profession is shown below:100% of East Londonderry Practices have Mental Health Practitioner  (88% have full MDT Mental Health entitlement)100% of East Londonderry Practices have Physiotherapy (88% have full MDT Physiotherapy entitlement)76% of East Londonderry Practices have Social Work (6% have full MDT Social Work entitlement)59% of East Londonderry Practices have Social Work Assistant  (47% have full MDT Social Work Assistant entitlement).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to spending at least 10 per cent of his budget on General Practice as suggested by the British Medical Association Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact of increasing demand and workload pressures.  I am clear in my view that General Practice is the bedrock of health and social care provision and I am committed to ensuring that General Practice is sustainable for the long term. Since returning as Health Minister, I have moved quickly to approve the Doctor’s and Dentists’ Review Body (DDRB) recommendation of a 6% uplift for 2023/24 for independent General Medical Practitioners.  In addition, I also approved an uplift of 6% for staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift in other expenses.In-year, my Department has provided significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services.  In October 2023, my Department published its Winter Pressures Plan, providing £3.4m in funding to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services to support the capacity of GP practices to meet the anticipated increase in demand over the winter period.  My Department also made £4.3m funding available to assist GP practices to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes this winter. In addition to this, the Department reallocated £2.7m as outlined above to boost capacity.  This support has been implemented alongside longer term measures such as maintaining the number of GP training places and ongoing investment in the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) Programme.While a budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging.  The Department is currently facing a significant number of competing funding challenges across Health and Social Care and will be in a clearer position to address specific funding pressures when the budget settlement is agreed.  Given this wider context, it is not possible for me to make long term spending commitments at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 8386/22-27, to provide a detailed breakdown of works required to address condition-based issues in Limavady Courthouse.
answer_text: The works required to address condition-based issues in Limavady Courthouse were assessed through a condition survey completed by external consultants, using standards published by the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS). That survey confirmed that there is a requirement to address condition-based maintenance issues in the majority of the building elements, broken down as follows:Renewal of the site perimeter fencing and general repairs to roads, paths, paving stones and concrete steps.Damp remediation and spalling damage repairs to brickwork and stonework.Repairs to clay roof tiles and lead work and replacement of fascia board, soffits and rainwater goods.Replacement of the majority of mechanical and electrical infrastructure, including fire detection system, intruder alarm, and low voltage distribution.Replacement of glazing units and cyclical redecoration of frames and doors.Replacement of light fittings and heat emitters.Refurbishment and redecoration of general areas including public ablutions and staff areas.Lifecycle replacement of floor finishes and internal doors.The extensive list of works above do not include the functional improvements and upgrades that are also required to provide suitable facilities for staff, victims, witnesses, defendants in custody, and disabled court users. Quantifying these functional improvements will form part of the estate strategy implementation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to establish a dedicated young patients fund, similar to the model which exists in Scotland.
answer_text: There are existing schemes in place to provide financial assistance to children and families in Northern Ireland who need to access healthcare. The Help with Healthcare Costs Scheme provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for health and social care treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist.  The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefits automatically passport on to the scheme.  In addition, should a child be sent outside of Northern Ireland to access specialist care and treatment, costs will be met for both parents to travel with the child. Development of any additional scheme would be dependent on additional recurrent funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has made an assessment of the financial impact on the families of children receiving long-term in-hospital treatment.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on discussions with ministerial colleagues on the development of an early learning and childcare strategy.
answer_text: I outlined my plans to Executive colleagues on 15th February for the development and implementation of an ambitious Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  I also obtained their commitment to support the work at an operational level by allocating appropriate staff resource to a cross-departmental Task and Finish Group, the first meeting of which is due to take place this week.  I have bid for funding to enable me to introduce a package of measures in the 2024/25 financial year.  My ability to implement these will be dependent on Executive colleagues agreeing to provide the budget required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the 2022 pilot programme set up in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust for the provision of assessment and diagnosis for adults with ADHD.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.A number of factors prompted the Northern Trust to progress with a small, unfunded pilot that saw those who have been referred to the Trust for Adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment, being placed on a waiting list and then being offered an appointment with the Trust. Part of the Pilot process is detailed pre-screening to ensure assessments are offered, where there is a high likelihood of Adult ADHD. All patients on any waiting list remain under the care of their GP.This pilot approach has taken time to establish given the preparatory work required. Communication with individuals on the register commenced in December 2023, in chronological order of date of original referral.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their key message to potential investors during their visit to Washington DC.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department intends to install traffic lights at Caw roundabout, Londonderry.
answer_text: As a result of significant housing development underway in the vicinity of the roundabout, a scheme to upgrade and signalise Caw Roundabout is currently conditioned to the new development.  My officials are continuing to engage with the developer to facilitate their delivery of these upgrades.  In addition, further upgrades to improve the overall safety and performance of this junction are also being considered by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: An Outline Business Case for Project Gigabit is in development and my Department continues to work with Building Digital UK on the development of a procurement approach for the project.  My Department is planning to award a contract for Project Gigabit in 2024, subject to necessary approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will provide additional funding to help stabilise community pharmacy services.
answer_text: I can assure the member that my Department is continuing to examine ways to address the financial challenges within community pharmacy. My Department is facing significant financial challenges and these remain at least into the short term. Additional investment of £10.1m provided for community pharmacy services in recent months was taken within the context of what was affordable. This does not mean that further interventions are not possible, however, these can only be made within a context of affordability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the current funding model for Community Pharmacy NI.
answer_text: I can assure the member that I have committed that further work will be taken forward in the coming months with Community Pharmacy NI, the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland, to review the current financial framework within current affordability and to consider the Scottish model of reimbursement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment on the future of Limavady courthouse.
answer_text: In December 2023, NICTS published its Estate Strategy, Making the Place for Justice.The Strategy is structured around 4 Improvement Aims and 12 Strategic Outcomes, that if realised, will result in a courts and tribunals estate that is capable of providing modernised and improved facilities for all citizens of Northern Ireland.To implement its Estate Strategy, NICTS will use data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes and develop prioritised investment delivery plans in the context of the resources available.Decisions on the future use of Limavady Courthouse will form part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how much public money has been spent to maintain Limavady courthouse since March 2020; and (ii) what investment is required for the facility to reopen.
answer_text: Limavady Courthouse has not been operational since March 2020, when my officials from the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) consolidated business delivery in the immediate aftermath of the Covid-19 pandemic.Prior to this, there were a number of known issues regarding the physical condition of the building. In the intervening period, NICTS has continued to undertake essential statutory and cyclical maintenance in Limavady Courthouse.  	Between April 2020 to December 2023, £96,135 has been incurred to address critical or statutory maintenance issues and to protect the building fabric in Limavady Courthouse.	The estimated costs to address the condition-based maintenance issues in Limavady Courthouse are £700k.  These costs do not include upgrade works to address the functional improvements that are also required.In December 2023, NICTS published its Estate Strategy, Making the Place for Justice. The Strategy is structured around 4 Improvement Aims and 12 Strategic Outcomes, that if realised, will result in a courts and tribunals estate that is capable of providing modernised and improved facilities for all citizens of Northern Ireland.To implement its Estate Strategy, NICTS will use data and evidence to baseline the existing estate against the agreed Strategic Outcomes, and develop prioritised investment delivery plans in the context of the resources available.Decisions on the future use of Limavady Courthouse will form part of this process.
