question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what progress has been made on recommendations for an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy; and (ii) what plans the Minister has to meet with the expert advisory panel.
answer_text: The Expert Advisory Panel’s recommendations have been considered by an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Co-Design Group. The Group have produced a draft strategy prioritised action plan. Owing to the cross-cutting nature of the strategy, a Cross-Departmental Working Group is now considering the proposed draft action plan and it will then be for Executive colleagues to consider and if content agree to issue for public consultation. The Expert Advisory Panel completed its work with the publication of a recommendations report in March 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with Executive colleagues to (i) replace the existing contract; and (ii) reinstate funding for free specialist adult counselling services provided by Nexus NI.
answer_text: My Department has been in communication with Nexus over many months in connection with the contract and has worked extensively with Nexus to improve waiting list times and the standard of service being provided.On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for specialist counselling services for victims of sexual abuse had not been withdrawn. I provided reassurance regarding my ongoing commitment to the importance of the provision of specialist counselling services for victims of sexual abuse.I also referred to the fact that I had asked my officials to extend the existing contract for another 12 months in order to allow them to examine potential alternative arrangements following the conclusion of the Nexus contract.However, having discussed the situation with officials, it was clear to me that further work is required on mapping out future sustainable arrangements and to allow more time for discussions with all colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what discussions his Department has had with counterparts in England regarding the introduction of baby loss certificates; (ii) how much it would cost his Department to introduce a similar certificate; and (iii) when he will make a decision on such a scheme.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what actions his Department is taking to support the work of Libraries NI.
answer_text: Notwithstanding the budgetary constraints in 2023/24, my department has provided just over 31 million pounds resource funding to Libraries NI which included over 3 million pounds to purchase book stock.My department has also provided Libraries NI with some 5.5 million pounds capital funding for 2023/24 to progress its IT infrastructure replacement programme and to commence construction of a new library facility in Fivemiletown.  I am also pleased to advise that my department will commence work on a new public libraries strategy to reflect the needs, challenges, and ambitions of a modern public library service.  I am mindful of the importance and value of our public library network, which provides safe, welcoming, and inclusive spaces for people to read, work, study, and access information and services.  Given the importance of our public library service to communities throughout Northern Ireland, my department will seek to provide the fullest support it can in terms of resource and capital funding investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to amend legislation to give councils more powers to deal with dilapidated buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why gully cleaning across East Londonderry is not carried out on a planned basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department is taking to reduce the sale of illicit drugs through social media platforms.
answer_text: Telecommunications legislation, including the regulation of social media platforms is a reserved matter.  The UK Government Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport is the lead Department for the draft Online Safety Bill. The general duty of care in the Online Safety Bill will require in-scope companies to put systems and processes in place to remove illegal content as soon as they become aware of it and take additional proactive measures with regards to the most harmful ‘priority’ forms of online illegal content; this would include the selling of illicit drugs and psychoactive substances.  Online companies will be required to prevent their services from being used to facilitate or commission the sale of illicit drugs and other designated priority offences.  Services will be required to minimise the length of time such content is accessible online and minimise the dissemination of it.  In Northern Ireland, the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) Drugs Sub Group provides a forum for engagement between the police and other stakeholders in response to the challenges posed by the misuse of drugs and drug-related threats, and to identify solutions to effectively deal with enforcement and supply.  The latest OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment sets out significant events in 2020/21 that impacted on the importation, distribution and sale of drugs by organised crime groups: https://www.octf.gov.uk/publications/octf-annual-report-and-threat-assessment-2020-21.  An example of the work of the OCTF Drugs Sub Group is its oversight of the Northern Ireland contribution to Operation Pangea, an annual global Interpol operation to safeguard public health, disrupt, and take down criminal activity relating to the trafficking of illicit pharmaceuticals and medical devices purchased online and distributed via the postal system.  This year, over a one-week period of action, the PSNI and Border Force seized over 242,000 illegal and unlicensed tablets and other unlicensed medicines destined for addresses throughout Northern Ireland. Operation Pangea is one specific operation; operational partners work tirelessly to disrupt all drug supply routes.  The PSNI Cyber Crime Department and Organised Crime Unit proactively investigate the sale of drugs online.  Operation Dealbreaker encompasses every aspect of drug related operational activity, the purpose of the ongoing operation is to detect and prevent those who supply, deal and financially benefit from illicit drugs supply and proactively work to break criminal drug dealing networks. While the justice system has a key role in stemming the supply of illegal drugs, I believe that we can only succeed in tackling substance misuse and improving outcomes for those affected by it, both directly and indirectly, through a multi-agency approach.  Preventative education for young and vulnerable people is essential, as is access to appropriate and timely therapeutic interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether web scraping technologies are currently in use in Northern Ireland for the detection of online crime.
answer_text: Criminal activity facilitated by the use of technology remains a feature of crime in Northern Ireland.  Further information about the threat from cyber crime and the response to the threat is set out in the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) Annual Report and Threat Assessment 2020/2021 – https://www.octf.gov.uk/publications/octf-annual-report-and-threat-assessment-2020-21.  The OCTF Cyber Crime Engagement Sub Group provides a forum for engagement between the Police Service of Northern Ireland, Government Departments and local government, relevant stakeholders across the private and Non-Governmental Organisation sectors, academia and cyber security industry in response to cyber crime and related developments, issues and threats.The methods used in the detection and investigation of crime, including online crime, are operational matters for the police and relevant law enforcement agencies.  As such you may wish to direct your question to the Police Service of Northern Ireland in the first instance. I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department have plans to train all medical and care staff in suicide prevention.
answer_text: The Regional Towards Zero Suicide (TZS) Programme aims to support the roll out of Suicide Prevention Learning across all HSCT Staff through work developed within the Workforce Development Workstream.A Learning Plan for HSC Staff to support suicide specific learning aligned to HSCT roles and accessible courses is being developed in two parts;Part 1 is around scoping of current, accessible and recommended learning for the range of Adult Mental Health roles across Trust services, and the development of a Suicide Prevention Learning and Support Plan for Adult Mental Health Service Staff.  This is almost complete with the exception of the monitoring system being fully integrated into ICT systems.  Part 2 includes the identification of appropriate and accessible Suicide Prevention Learning and Support Plan for wider/all HSCT staff including Medical and Nursing roles across Health and Social Care Trusts.  Attention to the development and implementation of this part will begin fully in April 2023 with the aim of a deliverable plan within resources but may require additional funding not yet secured.  To inform phase 2, two actions have commenced:  testing the level one Zero suicide online learning for broad application across on and off-site staff and a range of services – 11407 staff completed training across range of professions, services and divisions across HSC Trusts.  testing the introduction of mandatory suicide prevention awareness with a cohort of staff to identify if there are negative outcomes (e.g. elevating risk or negative impact in staff who are experiencing suicide ideation or behaviours, staff caring for someone experiencing suicide ideation or behaviours or staff bereaved by suicide).  This will guide the approach to scaling out learning more broadly across non-mental health staff in future.  Work continues with the Public Health Agency to take direction from the cross-sectoral Draft Mental Health Promotion and Suicide Prevention Learning Framework, to optimise utilisation of existing learning resources.  The resources required to support scale up of the learning plans across the system has yet to be defined and will determine the extent to which plans can be realised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what grants are available from her Department to help replace windows and doors in private dwellings.
answer_text: Private Sector Grants that would fund window and door replacements in private dwellings are;The Home Repair Assistance Grant or;The Renovation GrantHowever these two discretionary private sector grants administered by the Housing Executive are only ever awarded in exceptional circumstances and where the applicant meets the relevant eligibility criteria which can be found here:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Housing-Help/Grants/Types-of-grants-available.The Affordable Warmth Scheme may provide funding for replacement windows. The scheme is directed to those areas where levels of fuel poverty are highest.  It targets low income households, experiencing fuel poverty and energy inefficiency, in order to improve their home’s thermal comfort. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when patients will be able to book GP appointments in person at a GP Practice reception desk, rather than using the telephony service.
answer_text: It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate. The general practice telephone first consultations allow patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.  This approach helps the GP to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of us who seek clinical support and advice from our primary care practitioners.  I am aware that some patients are experiencing difficulties in relation to access to GP services and understand the frustration this can cause.  My Department has established a Working Group to explore issues around access to GP services, including demand for services, limitations of infrastructure, and staffing and resourcing issues.  The Working Group will also consider how technology can be better used to provide improved access in a standardised way across Northern Ireland.  The Working Group will propose actions to be taken in the short, medium and long term to improve access for patients.In recognition of the increasing demand for primary care services, my Department has continued to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I recently announced that my Department would be increasing of the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the review and subject to the availability of funding.It is important to note however that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Rather this is part of a wider programme of work to help improve patient access to services in primary care.  Other elements include the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams as well as the introduction of Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes.It will be important that Primary Care continues to transform to meet growing patient demand.  Technology and new ways of working will continue to play an important role alongside e

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans for additional social housing over the next five years in the (i) Limavady area; and (ii) Ballykelly area.
answer_text: I have developed a Housing Supply Strategy which sets out a long term framework for collaborative action to increase supply, including how we can deliver homes of different types to meet a range of housing needs. I am currently considering options for progressing the Strategy in the continuing absence of an Executive and agreed budgetThe Housing Executive will continue to work with housing associations on a scheme-by-scheme basis to ensure homes are provided where they are needed

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to funding and installing an MRI scanner at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust submitted a proposal for an MRI scanner at Causeway Hospital to my Department as part of the 10 year capital priorities review. In advance of an agreed budget my Department continues to evaluate what can be funded within the draft 2022-25 budget settlement and will keep this position under review. It is important to note that provision of new equipment necessarily entails the support of recurrent funding for the operational resources required to deliver the service. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care, and, as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. However, my ability to strategically plan capital investment is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Once this project becomes affordable, the Northern Trust will be invited to submit a business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will carry out repair and resurfacing work on the A2 from the City of Derry Airport to Campsey, to prevent further vehicles being damaged as a result of the road surface.
answer_text: The A2 Clooney Road, between Campsey and the City of Derry Airport at Longfield, forms part of the trunk road network. Decisions in relation to undertaking resurfacing works on the trunk road network are informed by analysis of data collected during our road testing programme. Analysis of the latest data indicates the threshold required for consideration of a resurfacing scheme has not yet been met, therefore my Department currently has no plans to carry out resurfacing works at this location.Due to ongoing budgetary constraints my Department is having to implement a limited maintenance service and therefore only the most serious of defects are currently being repaired. The continued boycott of the Executive by the DUP means over £420m of funds being held at the centre cannot be released and I therefore cannot bid for additional in year funding. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan future investment in our roads network.My officials advise that a number of actionable defects have been identified at this location and that the necessary repairs will be carried out over the next few weeks.
